title: Espionage Mission Hooks
description: The work behind the official work — extractions, dead drops, surveillance operations, asset management, disinformation campaigns, and the kind of jobs that require deniability more than documentation.

entries.result: An asset who has been in place for four years has gone silent. The last check-in was routine. The agency needs to know if she's blown, turned, or dead — in that order of preference.
A document needs to be in a specific diplomat's briefcase before she boards a plane in six hours. The document does not exist yet. The plane is on schedule.
A defector has made contact claiming to have time-sensitive intelligence. He's also made contact with two other agencies in the last forty-eight hours, which is either hedging or a trap.
A surveillance operation on a foreign trade delegation has picked up something unrelated to the original mission that is significantly more important than the original mission.
An extraction is needed for an operative who has been undercover for three years and whose cover has six hours before it breaks. The complication is that she doesn't want to come out.
A dead drop has been serviced, but by the wrong person. Whoever serviced it is now in possession of information that will accelerate a crisis the agency is trying to slow.
A mole hunt has narrowed to three candidates inside the agency. The operation to identify the mole must be run without any of the three candidates becoming aware they're suspects.
A foreign operative is being run against a domestic target and the agency has been tasked with burning the operation without alerting the foreign service that they've been identified.
An asset's handler has died, and the asset — active, productive, mid-operation — doesn't know this yet. Someone needs to reestablish contact under the existing cover before the asset goes dark.

A technical operation to install a listening device in a target facility requires a cover reason to be in the building, a technical specialist who can handle the device, and about four minutes unobserved.
A black site has been compromised and needs to be sanitized before a scheduled inspection. The sanitization needs to be complete. It also needs to leave no evidence of sanitization.
An asset has been turned and is now providing disinformation to the agency. Identifying which of his recent intelligence is accurate and which is manufactured before action is taken on any of it.

A political figure is about to make a decision based on intelligence that is technically accurate and operationally catastrophic. Changing the decision requires changing the intelligence without changing the record.

A courier carrying documents between two allied agencies has been intercepted. The intercept was by a third party. The documents need to be recovered before either of the original parties realizes they're missing.
An operation has produced evidence of a crime by a senior figure in a friendly government. The evidence is solid. Using it creates more problems than it solves. Not using it has its own costs.

A foreign intelligence service is running a recruitment operation against an agency employee who doesn't know it. Running a counter-operation requires letting the recruitment continue long enough to be useful.
A technical collection program has flagged communications that suggest a planned event. The communications are ambiguous. The window for action is seventy-two hours.
An asset has been arrested on an unrelated charge and is currently in a foreign detention facility. The charge is real but minor. The cover will not survive a thorough background investigation.

A disinformation campaign needs to be planted in the domestic press of a target country. The story needs to be credible, needs to source to someone who appears independent, and needs to run before a specific political event.
Two operatives in the same city are running separate operations that are about to intersect in a way neither of their handlers anticipated. Deconfliction must happen before the intersection.
A network that has been productive for eight years has been burned — all assets compromised — and the question of how it happened must be answered before rebuilding begins.
A foreign official is in the window where he's susceptible to recruitment: recent personal crisis, financial pressure, ideology not yet calcified. The window is approximately three weeks.
A hostile technical surveillance system has been installed in a facility the agency regularly uses. It needs to be located and removed in a way that doesn't reveal it was found.
An operation that was approved at the highest level has produced an outcome that was not approved. The documentation trail needs to be corrected before the review board meets in ten days.
A foreign intelligence officer is making unauthorized contact with an agency operative. It may be a genuine defection approach, a provocation, or a test of the operative's loyalty.
A product needs to reach a target's hands in a way that appears spontaneous — not delivered, not sent, not planted. The target must believe they found it.
An asset has been producing excellent intelligence for six months and the source protection analysis has concluded she cannot have legitimate access to what she's providing. Something is wrong.
A support network — safe houses, documents, communications — in a specific city needs to be rebuilt after a security review. The city is under heightened surveillance.
A planned operation has been leaked at the planning stage. Identifying the leak before the operation is modified or abandoned, without alerting whoever is responsible.

A technical exploit in a target government's communication system has been identified. Using it will produce significant intelligence. Using it will also reveal the capability, which has broader value.
An asset has died of apparently natural causes. A preliminary review suggests the causes were assisted. Determining whether this is true, and by whom, without signaling that it's being investigated.
A satellite passing over a target area has imaged something that analysts cannot identify. A ground team needs to get close enough to collect physical intelligence without alerting the target.
A trusted intermediary has been providing communications between two factions that the agency is running simultaneously. The intermediary has begun asking questions.
An operative's cover business needs to show legitimate activity to pass an upcoming tax audit. The audit is in two weeks. The business has been entirely non-functional for operational reasons.
A foreign service's counterintelligence division has opened a file on an asset. The file is thin — surveillance, not confirmation — but will become thicker unless something changes.
A technology that a target government believes is secret has been acquired by a third party. Both the target government and the third party are unaware the other knows. The agency is aware of both.
An operation in a denied area requires a communication path that doesn't rely on existing infrastructure. Building an alternative communication path before the operation launches.
A long-term asset is being considered for a promotion into a position of significantly higher access. The promotion needs to be supported without appearing to have been engineered.
A domestic operation has been run under an incorrect legal authority. Correcting the documentation before it's reviewed requires access to records that are held by someone who will ask questions.
A target has moved outside the operational area where the running agency has authority to act. The intelligence he carries doesn't wait for jurisdictional resolution.
An asset's family member has been identified by a hostile service as a potential pressure point. Moving the family member without revealing that the threat has been detected.

A foreign official attending an international conference needs to leave the conference with a different understanding of a specific issue than the one she arrived with. The mechanism for achieving this must be undetectable.
An encrypted communication has been partially decoded. The partial decode suggests a second operation is running parallel to a known one. The second operation has a deadline that has already passed.

A source has provided intelligence that contradicts a long-standing assessment. Determining whether the intelligence is accurate or the source has been compromised, without acting on either assumption.

An asset is being run by two separate handlers who are unaware of each other, the result of a compartmentalization failure. The duplication needs to be resolved without either handler learning about the other.
A target facility has been hardened against technical collection since a previous operation. Determining what changed and why without revealing that the original collection occurred.
A foreign government has identified a collection program and is feeding it deliberately falsified intelligence. Determining how much of the program's historical take is contaminated.

An operative under official cover has attracted personal attention from a counterintelligence officer who is acting outside his professional role. Determining whether the attention is personal or operational.
A financial operation designed to influence a target economy has had unintended consequences that are becoming visible. The connection to the agency must not become visible with them.
A joint operation with an allied service has produced intelligence that the ally wants to act on immediately. Acting on it will reveal a source that the agency values more than the immediate action.
A target's schedule for the next seventy-two hours needs to be altered without the alteration appearing deliberate. The mechanism must be realistic and leave no trace.
A technical operation requires physical access to a server room that has a two-person rule — no individual can be alone in the room. The cover for the second person must be airtight.
An asset has developed a drinking problem. Her access is currently intact. Her reliability is declining. The decision on how to handle this has been deferred three times.
A signals collection program has intercepted communication suggesting a planned assassination. The target is a foreign official with no relationship with the agency. The window is thirty-six hours.
An operation designed to run for six months is entering month nineteen with no end state in sight. Determining whether to continue, modify, or close it without losing what's been built.
A facility needs to burn — completely, verifiably — without appearing to have been burned deliberately. The timing must align with a plausible accidental cause.
A foreign intelligence officer has been making payments to a political campaign without the campaign's knowledge. Determining what she wants and whether to let her get it.

An operation requires a cover identity with five years of verifiable history in a city the operative has never visited. Building the history backwards from current date in time for a meeting in three weeks.

An asset has been identified as the likely source of a leak by a hostile service, but has not been arrested. The window between identification and arrest is the window for extraction, if that's the decision.
A recruitment has succeeded but the new asset is asking for guarantees that can't be given and won't move without them. Finding what can be offered that functions as a guarantee without being one.
A planned political event is being staged — the agency knows this — and its staging is real enough that it may produce genuine consequences if it runs.

A signals intercept has identified a location. The location is of significant intelligence value. Getting eyes on it without being detected requires traversing an area where the agency has no infrastructure.
An operative has been in cover so long she has begun making operational decisions based on her cover identity's interests rather than her actual mission objectives.
A hostile service has a source inside the agency at a level that can only be two or three people. Running a false flag operation to identify which one.
A technical collection device needs to be serviced while in place. Servicing it requires access to the facility. The facility's security posture has changed since installation.
An asset has requested a meeting outside the normal protocol. He's never done this before. Determining whether to respond, and how, without confirming the relationship to potential watchers.

A foreign service has placed a surveillance team on an operative who has done nothing to attract attention. The surveillance predates any operational activity. The source of the initial flag is unknown.
A covert influence operation has produced a political outcome that was not the intended one. The outcome is positive. The unintended nature of it creates a documentation problem.
An operation requires an operative to be present at a specific event without appearing to have been there. The event will have extensive photographic and video coverage.
A financial audit of an allied service's joint fund has turned up irregularities that suggest a hostile service has penetrated their financial infrastructure.
A long-dormant network has been activated by persons unknown, using communications protocols that were discontinued years ago. The activation is targeted, not broadcast.
An asset who was retired five years ago is being approached by a hostile service. She hasn't made contact with the agency. Determining whether she's going to before she makes a decision.
An operation producing significant intelligence has a methodology that would not survive legal scrutiny. The intelligence is currently being acted on. The scrutiny is incoming.

A foreign official's private communications have been obtained. The communications contain information that is personally damaging to the official but professionally irrelevant. Determining what to do with them.
A meeting between two parties needs to not happen. Preventing it without either party knowing it was prevented and without disrupting the relationship that would have produced the meeting.
A physical object needs to be removed from a secure facility without triggering an investigation into its absence. The absence will eventually be noticed. The timing of when it's noticed matters.
An operative's extraction plan has been compromised but the operative doesn't know it yet. Getting a new plan to her without using any of the compromised channels.
A government figure is being blackmailed by persons unknown, and is complying, and the compliance is affecting policy in ways that are visible to analysts but whose cause is not.
A joint operation with an allied service has concluded successfully but the allied service's account of events, which will be their permanent record, is significantly different from what happened.
An asset in a denied area needs documentation that cannot be sourced through any infrastructure the agency can access from outside the area. Someone needs to go in.

A hostile service operation against a third-party target is producing collateral harm to civilians. The agency has the capability to disrupt the operation. Disruption reveals the collection program that identified it.
A communication pattern analysis has identified a network that doesn't correspond to any known hostile service structure. It's operating inside a city the agency considers low-priority.
An operative's cover identity has been used by a separate party — not the agency — for an unrelated operation. The identity is now associated with an event the agency had nothing to do with.
A hostile service is about to arrest someone who is not an asset but who, if interrogated, will reveal information that will lead to an asset.
An operation requires compromising a specific individual. The compromise material must be developed, documented in a way that's credible, and delivered to a third party who will use it independently.
A recruitment target has agreed in principle but is asking for something the agency cannot provide and is unwilling to proceed without it.
A political figure is making decisions based on intelligence assessments that she doesn't know originate with the agency that is currently running an operation against her.
An asset has discovered that her handler has a personal financial interest in the intelligence she's providing. She hasn't confronted him. She's started asking different questions.
A physical surveillance package on a target has documented a meeting with a person who is not in any intelligence database. Identifying the second person is now the mission.
A foreign head of state's personal communications device has been identified as accessible. The access window is when she's in a specific building during a specific event. The event is in four days.
An exfiltration route that has been used successfully for years has been compromised. Three assets are currently in transit on that route.
A disinformation operation run by a hostile service has been identified, but exposing it publicly will reveal how it was identified, which is more valuable than exposing it.

An asset has been providing intelligence for three years. A recent audit suggests the intelligence has been accurate and the source protection has been excellent. Both facts are unusual enough to warrant scrutiny.
A hostile service has placed a technical device on an agency operative who doesn't know it's there. Removing it without revealing it was found. Using it to feed disinformation before removing it.
An operation has had its authorization chain broken by a personnel change. It is currently running without legal authority. Stopping it requires acknowledging it exists.

A defector has arrived with intelligence that is either the most significant acquisition in a decade or an elaborate fabrication designed to produce a specific response. Determining which under a seventy-two-hour deadline.
A covert action has gone further than authorized. The people who authorized it are no longer in their positions. The record of authorization is unclear. The action cannot be undone.

An asset has been in place so long and produced such consistent intelligence that no one has seriously questioned her loyalty in years. A new analyst has just asked a question that no one has an answer to.

A foreign service's counterintelligence chief has made an unauthorized approach to a senior agency figure, not through official channels, asking for a private meeting. She has identified herself accurately.
The operation was successful by every measurable criterion. The outcome is exactly what was intended. Six months later, someone is going to realize that the intended outcome was itself the operation.
